questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about earth's magnetism is correct?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statements about earth's magnetism is correct?

options.text: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic east to the west.
options.html: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic east to the west.

options.text: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south-east to the north-west.
options.html: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south-east to the north-west.

options.text: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.
options.html: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.

options.text: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south-west to the north-east.
options.html: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south-west to the north-east.

questions.correct_option_text: The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about bar magnet is correct?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statements about bar magnet is correct?

options.text: We cannot isolate the north and south pole of a bar magnet
options.html: We cannot isolate the north and south pole of a bar magnet

options.text: We can isolate the north and south pole of a bar magnet
options.html: We can isolate the north and south pole of a bar magnet

questions.correct_option_text: We cannot isolate the north and south pole of a bar magnet
questions.explanation: As magnetic monopole does not exist. If we split the bar magnet into two pieces, each part will have its own north and south pole.

questions.question_text: When iron filings are sprinkled on a sheet of glass placed over a short bar magnet then, the iron filings form a pattern suggesting that the magnet has
questions.question_html: When iron filings are sprinkled on a sheet of glass placed over a short bar magnet then, the iron filings form a pattern suggesting that the magnet has

options.text: south pole similar to the negative charge
options.html: south pole similar to the negative charge

options.text: north pole similar to the positive charge
options.html: north pole similar to the positive charge

options.text: two poles similar to the positive and negative charge of an electric dipole
options.html: two poles similar to the positive and negative charge of an electric dipole

questions.correct_option_text: two poles similar to the positive and negative charge of an electric dipole
questions.explanation: North pole act as positive end and South pole act as negative end. The iron fillings in the presence of magnetic field gets magnetized and form a tiny magnet which then gets attracted to the poles of bar magnet. North pole of bar magnet attracts south pole of tiny magnet and vice-versa. So all the iron fillings are arranged as the magnetic field lines of bar magnet.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statement about magnetic field lines is true?
questions.question_html: Which of the following statement about magnetic field lines is true?

options.text: The larger the number of field lines crossing per unit area, the weaker is the magnitude of the magnetic field B
options.html: The larger the number of field lines crossing per unit area, the weaker is the magnitude of the magnetic field B

options.text: The perpendicular to the field line at a given point gives direction of magnetic field
options.html: The perpendicular to the field line at a given point gives direction of magnetic field

options.text: The smaller the number of field lines crossing per unit area, the stronger is the magnitude of the magnetic field B
options.html: The smaller the number of field lines crossing per unit area, the stronger is the magnitude of the magnetic field B

options.text: The magnetic field lines of a magnet (or a solenoid) form continuous closed loops
options.html: The magnetic field lines of a magnet (or a solenoid) form continuous closed loops

questions.correct_option_text: The magnetic field lines of a magnet (or a solenoid) form continuous closed loops
questions.explanation: Outside the solenoid field lines moves from North to South pole, whereas inside it moves from South to North pole.

questions.question_text: In which case of comparing solenoid and bar magnet there is no exact similarity?
questions.question_html: In which case of comparing solenoid and bar magnet there is no exact similarity?

options.text: there is a current entering and a current leaving a solenoid
options.html: there is a current entering and a current leaving a solenoid

options.text: moving a small compass needle in the neighbourhood of a solenoid enables tracing the flux lines
options.html: moving a small compass needle in the neighbourhood of a solenoid enables tracing the flux lines

options.text: solenoid can be broken into two weaker solenoids
options.html: solenoid can be broken into two weaker solenoids

options.text: flux lines enter one end of a solenoid
options.html: flux lines enter one end of a solenoid

questions.correct_option_text: there is a current entering and a current leaving a solenoid
questions.explanation: The solenoid will change the field direction and the magnetic pole polarity as current direction is changed. But, the bar magnet will always have same poles, and it does not change.

questions.question_text: Magnetic field at far axial point due to solenoid as well as bar magnet varies
questions.question_html: Magnetic field at far axial point due to solenoid as well as bar magnet varies

options.text: inversely as cube of R, where R = axial distance from the magnet/solenoid
options.html: inversely as cube of R, where R = axial distance from the magnet/solenoid

options.text: inversely as R square, where R = axial distance to the magnet/solenoid
options.html: inversely as R square, where R = axial distance to the magnet/solenoid

options.text: directly as R squared, where R = axial distance to the magnet/solenoid
options.html: directly as R squared, where R = axial distance to the magnet/solenoid

options.text: directly as R cubed, where R = axial distance to the magnet/solenoid
options.html: directly as R cubed, where R = axial distance to the magnet/solenoid

questions.correct_option_text: inversely as cube of R, where R = axial distance from the magnet/solenoid
questions.explanation: Magnetic field at far off axial point due to a bar magnet as well as solenoid is given by {tex}\frac{\mu_o}{4\pi}\frac{2\overrightarrow m}{R^3}{/tex} , where m is magnetic moment and R is distance of axial point.

questions.question_text: Compass needle of magnetic moment m placed in a uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque equal to
questions.question_html: Compass needle of magnetic moment m placed in a uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque equal to
questions.explanation: Torque is vector quantity and is given by cross product of {tex}\overrightarrow m\times\overrightarrow B{/tex} Its magnitude is {tex}mBsin\theta{/tex} .

questions.question_text: Magnetic potential energy U m of compass needle of known magnetic moment m in a uniform magnetic field is
questions.question_html: Magnetic potential energy Um of compass needle of known magnetic moment m in a uniform magnetic field is
questions.explanation: The magnetic potential energy is negative times the scalar product of magnetic moment and the magnetic feild.

questions.question_text: According to the dipole analogy {tex}{\rm{1}}/{\varepsilon _0}{/tex} corresponds to
questions.question_html: According to the dipole analogy {tex}{\rm{1}}/{\varepsilon _0}{/tex} corresponds to
questions.explanation: {tex}{\mu _0}{/tex} in magnetisim is analogous to {tex}{\rm{1}}/{\varepsilon _0}{/tex} in electricity.

questions.question_text: According to Gauss’s law for magnetism -

options.text: The magnetic flux through an open surface is zero
options.html: The magnetic flux through an open surface is zero

options.text: The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is not zero
options.html: The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is not zero

options.text: The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero
options.html: The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero

options.text: The net magnetic flux through any surface is infinite
options.html: The net magnetic flux through any surface is infinite

questions.correct_option_text: The net magnetic flux through any closed surface is zero
questions.explanation: Total magnetic flux through a closed surface is zero as magnetic monoples do not exist. So the amount of magnetic flux entering a closed surface is equal to amount of magnetic flux leaving the surface.

questions.question_text: Considering the magnetic field of the earth, the correct relation of the geographic pole and the magnetic pole is
questions.question_html: Considering the magnetic field of the earth, the correct relation of the geographic pole and the magnetic pole is

options.text: north magnetic pole is located near the south geographic pole but south magnetic pole is not located near the north geographic pole
options.html: north magnetic pole is located near the south geographic pole but south magnetic pole is not located near the north geographic pole

options.text: north magnetic pole is located near the south geographic pole and south magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole
options.html: north magnetic pole is located near the south geographic pole and south magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole

options.text: north magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole
options.html: north magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole

options.text: north magnetic pole is not located near the south geographic pole but south magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole
options.html: north magnetic pole is not located near the south geographic pole but south magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole

questions.correct_option_text: north magnetic pole is located near the south geographic pole and south magnetic pole is located near the north geographic pole

questions.question_text: Nickel shows ferromagnetic property at room temperature. If the temperature is increased beyond curie temperature, then it will show
questions.question_html: Nickel shows ferromagnetic property at room temperature. If the temperature is increased beyond curie temperature, then it will show
questions.explanation: Above curie temperature, a ferromagnetic substance becomes paramagnetic.

questions.question_text: If the inclination is {tex}\theta{/tex} , the horizontal component of the earth’s field is H E and the vertical component of the earth’s field is Z E , then tan( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) is
questions.question_html: If the inclination is {tex}\theta{/tex}, the horizontal component of the earth’s field is HE and the vertical component of the earth’s field is ZE, then tan({tex}\theta{/tex}) is

questions.explanation: When magnetic field is passed through materials with non zero magnetization, generates field which themselves contributes internal field. So it is convenient to introduce another term "magnetic Intensity" (H), where H has the same dimensions as M and is measured in units of Am -1 . In this way we can find total magnetic field given by {tex}\overrightarrow B=\mu_o(\overrightarrow H+\overrightarrow{M)}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}\vec{H}{/tex} = magnetic intensity, {tex}\chi{/tex} = susceptibility, magnetic moment per unit volume {tex}\vec{M}{/tex} equals
questions.question_html: If {tex}\vec{H}{/tex} = magnetic intensity, {tex}\chi{/tex} = susceptibility, magnetic moment per unit volume {tex}\vec{M}{/tex} equals
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment per unit volume is called magnetization and it is given by {tex}\chi\vec{H}{/tex}
